skillName: Use formal methods for addition of decimals
misconceptionSummary: Students think trailing zeros after the decimal change the value when padding.

visuals.ruleText: Trailing zeros after the last significant decimal digit never change the value
visuals.subText: 12.4 = 12.40 = 12.400 — all the same point on the number line

steps.narration: Padding with trailing zeros is safe because zero hundredths adds nothing. Twelve point four and twelve point four zero are the same number — the zero just makes the column structure visible.

lines.text: Misaligned → wrong column values → wrong answer

steps.narration: Addition only works when matching place values are combined. A tenth added to a hundredth is not a valid column addition — they are different-sized units. Alignment guarantees like adds to like.

Estimate before calculating: twelve plus eight is twenty, so the answer should be close to twenty. The exact answer of twenty point two five passes that sense-check — a misaligned answer like ninety point nine clearly would not.

visuals.label: Estimate first → align points → pad → add

steps.narration: Twenty point two five. Estimate, align, pad, add — four steps that prevent the most common decimal addition errors.

misconceptionStrip.text: Common mistake: thinking 12.40 is different from 12.4 — it is not. Trailing zeros after the last decimal digit are invisible zeros that make column alignment possible without changing value.
misconceptionStrip.audioNarration: Padding with zeros does not change the number. Twelve point four and twelve point four zero sit on the same point on the number line — the zero just fills the column.
